title: A gardener's apron with pockets
subtitle: Heavy-cotton bib apron, tied at the waist, three patch pockets across the front
excerpt: A practical bib apron in cotton drill or canvas, sized to fit any adult, with three deep patch pockets across the front for secateurs, twine, gloves, and the running collection of pegs and labels that accumulates through a gardening day. Built from rectangles plus straps — no pattern pieces, all measurements in centimetres, machine or hand-sewn.
sourceNotes: WWII Make Do and Mend pamphlet (UK Board of Trade, 1943), pp 18-22, for the patch-pocket construction and the bib-and-waistband construction order — Crown Copyright expired. Beeton's Book of Needlework (1870), § Aprons, Project Gutenberg etext #25640, for the bib + waistband proportion (the bib width is the waist width minus 15 cm, the bib drop is 32 cm from waistband to top edge). Modern gardening-glove and tool dimensions used to size the pockets — secateurs 22 cm long, gardening gloves laid flat at 30 cm.

glossaryTerms.definition: A visible line of stitching close to a finished edge. Used to hold a fold flat, reinforce a seam, or add a decorative line. Stitched on the right side of the fabric with all-purpose thread (or heavier topstitch thread for emphasis) at a slightly longer stitch length than seam stitching — typically 3 mm.

A pocket cut as a separate piece and sewn onto the surface of a garment, rather than set into a seam. The simplest pocket construction — a rectangle of fabric with a folded-down top edge, hemmed sides and bottom, then stitched onto the garment along three sides with the top edge open.

A hem folded twice, so the raw edge is enclosed inside two layers of folded fabric. Sturdier and neater than a single fold; the standard hem for cotton drill, canvas, and any item that will be washed often. Typical measurements: first fold 1 cm, second fold 1.5 cm, total hem 2.5 cm.

content.text: A working apron is rectangles and straps. The bib protects the chest from soil and water spatter; the skirt covers the front from waist to knee; the patch pockets hold the working tools the day produces. This version is sized to fit any adult — the straps cross at the back and tie at the front, so the apron adjusts to the wearer without buttons or buckles. Made in cotton drill or canvas it will outlive several gardening seasons; in denim it will outlive every other garment in the wardrobe.

The construction is the order the WWII utility pamphlets used: hem the apron edges first, attach the pockets, then build the bib + waistband + straps as a single unit and stitch them onto the apron's skirt. Press at every stage; the apron looks worn-in within an afternoon of gardening but it has to start crisp.
What you'll need
Materials:
1.1 metres of medium- to heavy-weight cotton — cotton drill (around 270 gsm), cotton canvas (around 330 gsm), or denim. Standard 112 cm fabric width.
All-purpose polyester thread (Tex 27 / size 50), in a colour that matches or contrasts the fabric — drill and canvas take topstitching in a darker shade well.
1.5 metres of 25 mm-wide grosgrain ribbon for the waist ties (or cut self-fabric strips, see Variations).
Equipment:
Sewing machine with a size 90 universal or size 100 jeans needle. Drill and canvas are within the universal needle's range; denim needs the jeans needle.
Iron and pressing cloth (a thin cotton tea towel works).
Tape measure or metre stick, dressmaking scissors, tailor's chalk or wash-out marker, pins.
Before you start

Pre-wash the fabric. Cotton drill, canvas, and denim all shrink — typically 4-7% on the first hot wash. Wash on the hottest cycle the fabric will eventually be washed at (60°C for a gardening apron is realistic, given the mud), tumble-dry if you'll tumble-dry it in use, then iron flat. Cutting before pre-washing makes the finished apron 3-5 cm smaller than the pattern says.

Identify the right side of the fabric. On printed cotton drill the printed face is obvious; on solid fabrics the right side is usually the smoother face with a slightly tighter weave visible at the selvedge. Mark the right side with a pin in one corner. Identify the warp (lengthwise) and weft (crosswise) directions — the warp runs parallel to the selvedge.
All seams are 1 cm unless otherwise noted.
Cutting plan
Cut the following pieces, with the long dimension running along the warp (lengthwise of fabric). The total fabric needed is 1.1 m at 112 cm width.
Skirt: one rectangle 65 × 55 cm (the apron skirt — width × drop).
Bib: one rectangle 32 × 36 cm (width × drop from waistband to chest).
Waistband: two rectangles, each 67 × 8 cm.
Neck strap: one rectangle 4 × 70 cm (folded to make a 1.5 cm-wide strap).
Pockets: three rectangles, each 18 × 20 cm (width × drop).
The grosgrain ribbon waist ties are not cut — they attach at full length and you trim to fit after the apron is finished.
Construction
1. Hem the skirt
Start with the skirt rectangle. Fold and press a
double hem

(1 cm, then 1.5 cm) along the two side edges and the bottom edge. Pin. Machine straight-stitch close to the inner folded edge (about 2 mm in from the fold), backstitching at the start and end. Press the hems flat.
Leave the top edge of the skirt raw for now — it will be enclosed in the waistband at step 5.
2. Make the pockets

Take a pocket rectangle. The top edge — one of the 18 cm edges — gets a double hem: fold and press 1 cm, then 2 cm, then machine straight-stitch close to the inner fold. This is the pocket opening; making it stronger now is what stops the pocket gaping under the weight of secateurs.
On the other three edges, fold and press 1 cm to the wrong side. This is the seam allowance that turns under when the pocket is sewn onto the skirt. Pin the folds to hold them.
Repeat for the other two pockets. Press each finished pocket flat.
3. Position and stitch the pockets
Lay the skirt flat, right side up. Measure 15 cm down from the raw top edge (the waistband edge) and mark a horizontal line across the skirt — this is the top edge of the pocket row.

Position the three pockets along this line. Centre the middle pocket on the skirt's centre line. Space the side pockets so there is a 4 cm gap between each pocket and its neighbour — measure carefully, because uneven spacing reads as untidy.
Pin each pocket in place with pins through both layers. Machine
topstitch

each pocket onto the skirt at 2 mm from the pocket edge, along the two sides and the bottom — leave the hemmed top edge open (that's the pocket opening). At each top corner, sew a small triangle reinforcement: stitch from the corner inwards at 45 degrees for 1 cm, then back, then continue down the side. Two small triangles per pocket are the reinforcement that stops the corners ripping under heavy use.
Press the skirt flat from the wrong side.
4. Make the bib + neck strap
The bib top and the two sides are hemmed; only the bottom edge (the waistband edge) is left raw.
Fold and press a double hem (1 cm, then 1.5 cm) along the top edge of the bib and along the two sides. Machine straight-stitch each hem at 2 mm from the inner fold.

Make the neck strap: take the 4 × 70 cm rectangle, fold it in half lengthwise (right sides together) to make a 2 × 70 cm tube. Machine straight-stitch along the long open edge at 5 mm. Trim the seam allowance to 3 mm. Turn the tube right side out — a chopstick or wooden skewer through one end helps work the tube. Press the strap flat with the seam running along one edge.

Attach the strap ends to the top corners of the bib. On the wrong side of the bib, place one end of the strap at the top-left corner so that 2 cm of strap overlaps the bib corner. Stitch a 2 × 2 cm square across the overlap, then sew an X across the square — the box-and-X reinforcement is the bag-strap standard and holds against heavy pulling. Repeat at the top-right corner with the other end of the strap.
5. Join skirt and bib with the waistband

Take the two waistband rectangles (67 × 8 cm each). These form a sandwich that encloses the raw top edges of both the skirt and the bib. Press 1 cm to the wrong side on the two long edges of each waistband rectangle. Press 1 cm to the wrong side on the two short ends.

Place one waistband rectangle right-side up. Lay the skirt on top, right-side up, with the raw top edge of the skirt aligned 1 cm inside the top long edge of the waistband (i.e. matching the pressed fold). Centre the skirt: the waistband extends 5 cm beyond the skirt on each side — these are the ribbon-tie attachment zones. Pin.

Lay the bib on top of the skirt-and-waistband, right-side up, with the raw bottom edge of the bib aligned to the same line as the top edge of the skirt (so bib and skirt meet at the waistband centre). Centre the bib on the waistband. Pin.

Lay the second waistband rectangle on top, wrong-side up, aligning all four pressed edges with the first waistband. The skirt and bib raw edges are now sandwiched between the two waistband layers. Pin through all layers.

Machine straight-stitch around the entire perimeter of the waistband sandwich, at 2 mm from the edge. Sew through all layers — the seam closes the sandwich and locks the skirt + bib in place. At the 5 cm tie-attachment zones (where there is no skirt or bib between the waistband layers), continue stitching to close the waistband shut so the ends are finished and ready to take the ribbon ties.
6. Attach the ribbon ties

Cut the grosgrain ribbon in half, giving two 75 cm lengths. At one end of each ribbon length, fold 1 cm under and press. Position one ribbon at each end of the waistband — the folded ribbon end overlaps the waistband end by 2 cm. Pin.

Machine stitch a 2 × 2 cm square with an X across each ribbon attachment, the same box-and-X reinforcement used at the neck-strap corners. The waist ties take real load — the X reinforcement is what stops them ripping out.

Finish the free end of each ribbon: fold 1 cm under, then 1 cm again, and topstitch close to the inner fold. (Alternatively, cut the ribbon at 45 degrees and seal the cut edge with a small dab of fabric glue — that's the dressmaker's tailored finish.)
Final press + how to wear
Press the finished apron from the wrong side. The waistband is bulky with multiple layers — press from both sides if needed and steam to settle the seams.

To wear, slip the neck strap over the head and let the bib fall against the chest. The waist ribbons go around the back, cross at the spine, and tie in a bow at the front above the pocket row. The cross-at-the-back gives the apron a snugger fit and stops the bib sliding sideways during work.
Variations

Self-fabric ties: instead of grosgrain ribbon, cut two strips 6 × 75 cm from the apron fabric. Fold each strip in half lengthwise right sides together, stitch at 5 mm along one short end and the long open edge, trim and turn through. Press. Attach to the waistband ends as for the ribbon version.

Pocket variations: two larger pockets (each 25 × 22 cm) instead of three — better for a bib-pocket tool roll. Or a divided pocket: stitch a vertical line through the middle of the centre pocket to split it into two 8 cm-wide compartments — useful for separating secateurs from a notebook.
Child's size: scale every dimension to 70% of the adult version. The 65 × 55 cm skirt becomes 46 × 39 cm; the 32 × 36 cm bib becomes 22 × 25 cm; the pockets become 13 × 14 cm.
Care

Cotton drill, canvas, and denim all take a 60°C wash with a cool tumble or line dry. Iron flat between uses if the apron will go straight from the line into the wardrobe. A gardener's apron does not need ironing daily; the creases are part of the look.
Troubleshooter

attrs.heading: If something went sideways
attrs.intro: The common ways the gardener's apron under-performs in construction, and the fix.

items.symptom: Pockets gaping open under the weight of secateurs
items.cause: Pocket opening not double-hemmed strongly enough; or no corner reinforcement.
items.fix: Re-hem the pocket opening with a double 2 cm hem if the original is 1 cm. Sew the X-reinforcement triangles at the top pocket corners — that is the load-bearing detail.

items.cause: Skirt or bib not aligned to the pressed fold of the waistband; or skirt's raw edge not trimmed straight before insertion.
items.fix: Unpick the perimeter waistband stitching. Trim the skirt's raw edge straight. Re-align skirt and bib raw edges exactly to the pressed 1 cm fold of the waistband. Press all four pressed folds again before re-stitching.

items.symptom: Neck strap pulling out at the box-and-X reinforcement
items.cause: Box-and-X stitched only through the strap and one bib layer.
items.fix: Reinforce with a second box-and-X through both bib layers — the bib's top hem is doubled so there are two layers there, and the reinforcement must catch both. Use heavier topstitch thread on this single detail if available.

items.symptom: Topstitching wandering away from the edge
items.cause: Foot pressure too light for the fabric weight; or going too fast.
items.fix: Use the edge of the presser foot as the guide and sew slowly. A topstitching foot or an edge-stitch foot with a built-in guide locks the line at a precise distance.

items.symptom: Apron skirt twisting on the body when worn
items.cause: Waistband not centred on the skirt — one side has a longer tie zone than the other.
items.fix: Unpick and re-stitch the waistband sandwich so the 5 cm tie zones are equal at each end. Mark the centre of the waistband and the centre of the skirt with chalk before pinning.
